meta.ship: The St. Roch & the Panikpakuttuk Family
meta.subtitle: Two journeys through the Northwest Passage — the ship’s, and the Inuit family’s two-year way home (1940–1946)
meta.blurb: The RCMP schooner St. Roch crossed the Northwest Passage in both directions — west‑to‑east in 1940–1942, then the record single‑season return in 1944. This map follows both voyages and, in parallel, the Panikpakuttuk family: the Inuit family hired at Mittimatalik (Pond Inlet) whose hunting, sewing and navigation made the 1944 crossing possible, who were then left to winter alone, and who took two years to find their way home.
note: This map loads its chart tiles from the web, so it needs an internet connection to draw the basemap.

Both course lines are hand‑drawn approximations routed to stay in water through the historical channels. In the narrowest straits — Bellot, Prince of Wales, the Bering Strait — they are not survey‑accurate.
Anchorages, dates, and log notes come from the two source KML files. The 1944 file dates the final Vancouver arrival as 1945; that is a typo, corrected here to 16 October 1944.
The Panikpakuttuk family’s two‑year journey home (1944–1946) is a separate overlay; several of its points are best‑estimate reconstructions from the historical record and are marked as estimated.

groups.sub: Westward from the Pacific into the Beaufort ice

groups.title: Winter 1940–41
groups.sub: Frozen in at Walker Bay, Victoria Island
Through Coronation Gulf to King William Island

groups.title: Winter 1941–42
groups.sub: Frozen in at Pasley Bay, Boothia Peninsula
Bellot Strait, Lancaster Sound, and home to Halifax

stops.name: June 9, 1940 - HMC Naval Dockyard Equimalt BC
desc: St.Roch Left the HMC Naval Dockyard in Esquimalt.
St.Roch when not in service would port either At the HMC Esquimalt Naval Dockyard or in Vancouver.

stops.name: June 9, 1940 - Vancouver, BC
desc: Left Vancouver on June 23rd, 1940 at 2:50 AM
Colemans and Evans Wharf for Imperial Oil float.

stops.name: July 5, 1940 - Akutan Harbour
desc: Anchored at Akutan Cove awaiting favourable weather

stops.name: July 6, 1940 - Dutch Harbour
desc: July 8, 1940 St.Roch loaded fresh vegatables and eggs and refueled 2,153 gallons of oil.

stops.name: July 22, 1940 - Utqiagvik (Cape Smyth), Alaska
desc: July 22, vessel headed northward but forced to anchor at Point Barrow due to weather.

stops.name: August 12, 1940 - Hershel Island
desc: At Hershel Island loaded supplies to be delivered to Tuktoyaktuk for the Aklavik Detachment.

stops.name: August 20, 1940 - Tuktoyaktuk, NT
desc: Unloaded supploes for the Aklavik detachment and transfer of crew.

stops.name: August 31, 1940 - Kugluktuk (Coppermine, NU )
desc: Kugluktuk (Inuinnaqtun: Qurluqtuq), which translates to "place of moving water" or "place of rapids".

stops.name: September 8, 1940 -  Iqaluktuuttiaq(Camrbidge Bay, NU)
desc: St. Roch left Cambridge Bay on September 10th.
Iqaluktuuttiaq (or Ikaluktutiak) , which translates to "good fishing place"

stops.name: September 10, 1940 - Finlayson Islands, Kitikmeot Region, NU
September 13, 1940 - Tree River
desc: Anchored at Tree River due to strong Easterly Gale.

stops.name: September 20, 1940 - Holman Island
desc: Anchored at Holman Island off Hudon's Bay Company Post.
September 21, 1940 - Received Instructions by wireless to proceed to Walker Bay for winter Quarters.

stops.name: September 22, 1940 - De Salid Bay
desc: Considered for winter quarters but the harbour is to large.

stops.name: September 26, 1940 to July 31, 2941 - Walker Bay (WINTER QUARTERS 1940)
desc: Ship at Walk Bay for winter quarters from September 26, 1940 to July 31, 1941.
Patrols too: Holman Island, Minto Inlet, Ramsay Island, Victoria Island, Princess Royal Island and Banks Island.

stops.name: July 31, 1941 - Holman Island
desc: Picked up Jack Goose who required medical attention in Aklavik.

stops.name: August 2, 1941 - Cape Bathurst
desc: Anchored due to dense fog on the way to Aklavik.

stops.name: August 12, 1941 - Kugluktuk (Coppermine), NU
desc: Unloaded supplies for Coppermine Detachment supplies.
Left Coppermine on August 14, 1941 for Cambridge Bay.

stops.name: September 3, 1941 to August 3, 1942 - Pasley Bay, Boothia Peninsula
desc: September 3, 1941 St.Roch enters Pasley Bay, Boothia Penisula to avoid incoming ice. Conditions worsen despite efforts of St.Roch to push forward. By September 11, 1941 the inlet had frozen over and St.Roch prepared to Winter in Pasley Bay until summer 1942.
August 3, 1942 St.Roch left Pasley Bay. The decision was made in a hurry to avoid the risk of worsening conditions and having to winter another year.

stops.name: August 26, 1942 - Tasmania Islands, Kitimeot Region
desc: August 26, 1942 St.Roch anchored between Tasmania Islands of Kitikmeot Region.

Between Pasley Bay and Tasmania Island St.Roch experienced unfavourable conditions and engine issues until a strong northerly gale presented an opening in the ice that allowed St.Roch to push forward.

stops.name: August 29, 1942 - Dixon Island
desc: Considtion favourable and able to conitnue westerward.

stops.name: August 30, 1942 - Fort Ross
desc: Anchored at Fort Ross, Hudson's Bay Outpost.

stops.name: September 6 , 1942 - Mitimatilik (Pond Inlet)
desc: Dogs put ashore at this detachment.

stops.name: September 30, 1942 - Corner Brook, Newfoundland
desc: Anchored at Corner Brook, Newfound Land
St.Roch required fresh water and temporarily fixes engine cylinder.

stops.name: October 8, 1942
desc: Anchored at Sydney Harbour, Cape Brenton Island.

groups.title: 1944 · Atlantic & Baffin Bay
groups.sub: Halifax up through Davis Strait to Pond Inlet

groups.title: 1944 · The Parry Channel
groups.sub: The deep northern route — Lancaster Sound to Melville Island

groups.title: 1944 · Western Arctic & home
groups.sub: Prince of Wales Strait, the Beaufort coast, and the Pacific

stops.name: July 23, 1944 - Sydney, Cape Breton Island
desc: Anchored at Sydney Naval Dockyard, Cape Breton Island

stops.name: August 12, 1944 - Mitimatalik (Pond Inlet)
desc: Unloaded supplies to be delivered to the detachment and loaded St.Roch cache supplies from 1942.
Panikpakuttuk hired as a guide and is joined by his family.

stops.name: August 18, 1944 - Dundas Harbour
desc: Sheltered here from poor visibility and wind.
Found buildings in good shape and left records of their visit.

stops.name: August 20, 1944 - Beechey Island
desc: Beechey Island is the wintering site of Sir John Franklin. (https://www.pc.gc.ca/apps/dfhd/page_nhs_eng.aspx?id=809)

stops.name: August 25, 1944 - Byam Maritin Island
desc: Anchored at NE Point of Byam Maritin Island.

stops.name: August 30, 1944 to September 3, 1944
desc: St.Roch sighted Prince of Wales Strait on August 31, 1944 and Collisen Inlet on September 3, 1944.
low visibility, a broken Gyro-scope and heavey ice left the crew unsure of their exact location.

stops.name: September 4, 1944 - Walker Bay
desc: Passed Walker Bay, Previous Winter Location for St.Roch in 1940 to 1941.

stops.name: September 10, 1944 - Tuktoyaktuk
desc: Awaited favourable conditions before proceeding to Herschel Island.

stops.name: September 18, 1944 - Herschel Island
desc: Dropped off Panikpakuttuk and Family to live at abandoned RCMP outpost on Herschel Island for the winter (September 17, 1944 to August 13, 1945)

stops.name: September 27, 1944 - King Island
desc: Anchored overnight off village site at King Island

stops.name: October 1, 1944 - Akutan Harbour
desc: Anchored at Akutan Harour U.S. Navy Fuel Base.

stops.name: October 16, 1945 - Coleman Evans Warf, Vancouver
desc: Moored at Coleman Evans Wharf, Vancouver BC.

stops.title: The family comes aboard
stops.note: Mittimatalik, 15 August 1944. Larsen hires Joe Panikpakuttuk — informally, paid only in rations, never made a Special Constable, never put on the paylist. Eight people across three generations come aboard with about seventeen dogs and pitch a sealskin tent on the cargo hatch; the Guidebook records that this marked them as “second class citizens.” Joe: “The total of us was eight.”

stops.title: Beechey Island — Franklin & the cylinders
stops.note: 20 August 1944. The Franklin graves and cenotaph; Larsen reads the old exploration cairns and the crew build cairns of their own, leaving brass cylinders that record Canadian administration of the land. Joe, on the lost Franklin expedition: it “was never found.”

stops.title: Joe’s walrus hunt feeds the ship
stops.note: 22–24 August 1944. With the canned rations nearly inedible, it is Joe who feeds the ship. Diplock: Panikpakuttuk was “chiefly responsible for shooting the walruses,” and taught the crew how to slice down through the hide and fat. The meat fed both the men and the dogs.

stops.title: “The rocks began to move”
stops.note: 27–28 August 1944. Parry’s 1819–20 wintering place and Kellett’s 1852–53 cache. Here Joe sees musk oxen for the first time: “All I could see were huge rocks. Mr. Larsen said these were musk oxen… So I looked again through the telescope and the rocks began to move.”

stops.title: Panikpak finds the ship
stops.note: 30–31 August 1944, the entrance to Prince of Wales Strait. In fog, Larsen cannot tell Russell Point from Peel Point and takes the ship up the wrong inlet. Joe’s mother, the elder Panikpak, reads the coastline against the chart and locates the ship — “That’s where we are,” she said — “despite the disbelief of the crew.” The single most important Inuit-knowledge moment of the voyage.

stops.title: First strangers since home
stops.note: 4 September 1944. Joe’s first people since Pond Inlet: “we began to feel shy… I shook hands.” When a crew member steals a narwhal tusk and tries to blame him, Joe tells the truth and Larsen takes the Inuk’s word over his own crew’s — a rare inversion of power. The crew, the same day: “we’d gotten through the part of the Passage that nobody else had.”

stops.title: The great storm
stops.note: 8–17 September 1944, a nine-day gale. Joe: “the boat rocked as though it were going to turn over.” Mary Panigusiq, aged eight: “I hated the trip… it was terrible living in the tent on the deck. The water would come right into the tent and I got scared.” Suupi, aged four, remembered the spare rudder breaking loose and her father’s handprints pressed into it.

stops.title: Left behind — the lonely winter
stops.note: Herschel Island (Qikiqtaruk), 17–21 September 1944. The St. Roch lands the family at the deserted station and sails on toward its record, reaching Vancouver on 16 October 1944. They had never been told how long it would be, or that they would have to find their own way home. Joe: “He left and we were alone there.” Mary: “It was lonely and new and strange. It was terrible.” Through the winter Joe and his stepson Arreak hunted caribou and trapped twelve foxes; Joe sledged to Aklavik and back (nine sleeps); by spring he was taking sixteen to eighteen seals a day. They would not be picked up until the next August.

home.title: Wintering alone at Herschel
home.note: For almost a year the family lived alone at the abandoned station. The St. Roch finally returned on 11 August 1945 — but only to carry them deeper into the Arctic, still not home.

home.phase: 1945 · picked up by the St. Roch
home.title: Why the ship came back — and carried them west
home.note: The St. Roch had reached Vancouver in October 1944, but its duty was the western Arctic patrol. In the summer of 1945 it sailed north again from the Pacific coast, and on 11 August 1945 that patrol reached Herschel and took the family aboard. They expected to go home east. Instead the 1945 supply route ran the other way — east and south, deeper into the Arctic — and the ship called here at Tuktoyaktuk on the way.

home.title: Coppermine & the 39-walrus hunt
home.note: Through Amundsen Gulf and Dolphin & Union Strait to Coppermine. In Coronation Gulf the season’s communal hunt took thirty-nine walruses, Inuit hunting from kayaks and umiaks; about twenty tons of meat were prepared ashore for a local camp and the rest kept as dog feed for the winter to come.

home.title: A second winter — then the sled leaves from here
home.note: A whole second winter beside the ship. Joe and Larsen’s under-ice net fishery put up an estimated 1,085 fish for the dogs. On 18 December 1945 the Polar Medal was recommended for the 1944 crew — the crew that had left Joe off the list — while the family it omitted wintered a few yards away. The dog-sled journey home finally began from this point on 22 April 1946.

home.title: Gjoa Haven — the guides change
home.note: They left Cambridge Bay with eight grown dogs and five pups on a twenty-foot sled, guided first by Kanayuk. At Gjoa Haven the family was handed to the young Netsilik guide Tiitaa and his wife. From here on, Inuit guided Inuit home.

home.title: Up the Boothia coast
home.note: Estimated. North along the Boothia Peninsula and out over the sea ice of the Gulf of Boothia, the only road home. The exact track is not recorded; this is a reconstruction from the route the journey is known to have taken.

home.place: Gulf of Boothia sea ice (Tiitaa leg)
home.title: With the guide Tiitaa
home.note: Estimated. Tiitaa and his wife led the family across the sea ice toward Igutalik and Ikirasak, travelling with another Inuit family, sharing snow-houses and hunting as they went — the journey home was itself an act of Inuit community.

home.title: The crisis
home.note: Estimated. Bad weather pinned them down; for two days there was no food and Joe fell gravely ill. At dawn a polar bear walked into camp. “I unchained all the dogs and let them run after the bear but when I tried running I was so weak that I kept falling down. The dogs finally stopped the animal and I shot it.” Then two more days in bed before they could move on.

home.title: Fort Ross — the sled ends, board the ship here
home.note: They reached the Hudson’s Bay Company post at Fort Ross over the sea ice, and the dog-sled journey ended here. The last stretch home was open water, so they waited at Fort Ross for a ship — and it was from here that they finally boarded the HBC supply vessel Nascopie for the run home.

home.title: Aboard a different ship — north up Prince Regent Inlet
home.note: Estimated track. This is the second ship of the family’s story. From Fort Ross the Nascopie carried them north up Prince Regent Inlet; the change of ship is why this part of the line is drawn in a different colour.

home.title: East through Lancaster Sound
home.note: Estimated track. The Nascopie ran east through Lancaster Sound — the great gateway of the eastern Arctic — and turned south toward home waters the family had not seen in over two years.

home.title: Home, more than two years later
home.note: September 1946. Home at last, more than two years out of a voyage celebrated as “86 days.” The community, having heard nothing for two years, had assumed the family dead, and met them with surprised tears. Joe at first found it hard to speak with his own people: “I kept talking in Western dialect, I had been away that long.”

side.title: Joe’s trip to Aklavik (nine sleeps)
side.note: Estimated, and a detour rather than the route home. During the lonely first winter, Joe sledged from Herschel to Aklavik and back — nine sleeps — and discovered that Western Arctic Inuit paid each other cash for meat, something new to him; he earned about $200.
